title: Early Mortgage Renewal — When Breaking Early Pays Off
metaTitle: Early Mortgage Renewal in Canada — Worth the Penalty?
metaDescription: When does breaking your mortgage early to renew at a lower rate actually save money? Penalty calculation, break-even analysis, and lender 'blend and extend' options.
intro: An early renewal means breaking your existing term to lock a new one — usually because rates have dropped meaningfully since you signed. Sometimes it's brilliant, sometimes the penalty wipes out the savings. The math is the only thing that decides.

body.heading: How the penalty works
paragraphs: On a fixed-rate mortgage, breaking early triggers the greater of three months' interest or the Interest Rate Differential (IRD). On a variable-rate mortgage, it's almost always three months' interest only — much cheaper. That's why variable-rate holders have far more flexibility to renew early when rates drop.

Big banks compute IRD using their posted rate (the headline rate they advertise) which inflates the penalty dramatically. A typical big-six early-break penalty on a $700,000 Greater Vancouver mortgage can run $20,000–$40,000. Monoline lenders use the discounted rate they actually gave you, producing penalties usually 60–80% lower.

body.heading: Three ways to renew early without breaking
paragraphs: Option A: 'Blend and extend.' Most lenders will blend your existing rate with current rates and extend your term. No penalty, but the new rate is a weighted average — not the headline current rate. Useful when rates have dropped slightly. Option B: Wait until 120 days before maturity, when most lenders will let you lock the new rate without penalty. Option C: Refinance to a different lender at maturity — same as a regular renewal, with the option to take cash out at the same time.

faqs.question: How do I find out my exact penalty?
faqs.answer: Call your lender and request a written 'payout statement' or penalty quote. Verbal quotes can be off by thousands. The quote is good for 30 days typically.

faqs.question: When does breaking early make sense?
faqs.answer: When your savings over the new term clearly exceed the penalty plus legal/appraisal costs, and you'll stay in the mortgage long enough to capture them. The Refinance Calculator on this site does the math.

faqs.question: Is blending always better than breaking?
faqs.answer: Only if the blended rate is competitive. Blends are quoted by your current lender — and their incentive isn't to give you the best rate. Always compare blend-and-extend against breaking-and-switching before deciding.
